AU-01HIGH
Mobile visitors are not told who the store is built for
1Turn the mobile hero into a faster first decision
Fixes 3 findings across 2 clusters (visual-cta, audience)
Add a clear headline and audience line above the fitment stack, then reduce the vertical footprint of the four-field flow so the page promise and first merch cue appear sooner.
Underlying findings
visual-cta/F-01 — Mobile hero still lacks a visible promise before the fitment flow
visual-cta/F-02 — The stacked fitment flow consumes the first decision window
audience/F-01 — Mobile visitors are not told who the store is built for
2Move reassurance into the mobile browse path instead of the footer
Fixes 4 findings across 3 clusters (trust-credibility, pricing, checkout-flows)
Add a compact mobile trust/value strip under the hero and reinforce it on the first product cards with reviews, shipping or lead-time guidance, returns, and express checkout reassurance.
Underlying findings
trust-credibility/F-01 — The mobile CTA has almost no adjacent reassurance
trust-credibility/F-02 — Social proof in the featured carousel feels patchy
pricing/F-01 — Mobile product cards show price without enough delivery context
checkout-flows/F-01 — Express-checkout reassurance is hidden until the footer
3Make mobile comparison easier once shoppers reach products
Fixes 3 findings across 3 clusters (performance-ux, product-media, category-navigation)
Shorten the top stack and redesign the first mobile merch module for faster scan-and-compare behavior with stronger card evidence and clearer category continuation.
Underlying findings
performance-ux/F-01 — Mobile comparison starts too late and scans too slowly
product-media/F-01 — Quick view and a single image do too much of the card's selling work
category-navigation/F-01 — Category routes appear after the fitment stack instead of accelerating it
4Upgrade homepage discoverability and lifecycle confidence
Fixes 2 findings across 2 clusters (content-seo, post-purchase)
Rewrite the homepage metadata around the real vehicle/value proposition and turn the lower-page retention area into a support-and-next-step block first, email capture second.
Underlying findings
content-seo/F-01 — Mobile still inherits weak homepage metadata and structure
post-purchase/F-01 — Lower-page retention still centers email capture over ownership confidence
No ethics findings.

Ethics findings are BLOCK (hard violations) or ADJACENT (one step from a violation). A clean slate here means nothing in the audit triggered the ethics gate.

VC-01HIGHHeadlines & Buttons
Mobile hero still lacks a visible promise before the fitment flow

Element

hero-fitment

Observation

The first mobile screen is dominated by the vehicle selector stack, but there is still no visible homepage headline, subhead, or explicit promise clarifying what AWDMods offers before the shopper starts filling fields.

Recommendation

Add a concise H1 and support line above the fitment stack so mobile visitors understand the category focus before they invest thumb effort.

Why this matters

Mobile users decide even faster than desktop users whether the page is relevant, so the promise cannot hide behind a multi-step form. â†³ Ecommerce heroes work best when the value proposition is visible immediately instead of requiring interaction to discover the offer. [Gold]

Source

Hero Section Psychology
Open primary source ↗
VC-02HIGHHeadlines & Buttons
The stacked fitment flow consumes the first decision window

Element

hero-cta

Observation

Mobile requires four stacked dropdowns plus a CTA before the shopper reaches any meaningful browsing module, which makes the homepage feel like a task flow first and a storefront second.

Recommendation

Compress the fitment module, consider progressive disclosure, and allow product/category discovery to appear earlier on the first two screens.

Why this matters

When the first usable shopping cue is delayed by a tall form stack, casual visitors lose momentum before the site proves its inventory depth. â†³ Above-fold mobile real estate should prioritize immediate comprehension and the fastest route into meaningful browsing. [Gold]

Source

Hero Section Psychology, Page Performance Psychology
Open primary source ↗
TC-01HIGHTrust & Credibility
The mobile CTA has almost no adjacent reassurance

Element

hero-cta

Observation

The mobile `FIND PARTS` CTA is easy to read, but it is not paired with review proof, delivery reassurance, return policy cues, or payment confidence directly around the hero stack.

Recommendation

Add a mobile trust strip under the CTA with reviews, shipping promise, and a plain-language support or returns cue.

Why this matters

Small screens compress attention, so reassurance has to travel with the primary action instead of living elsewhere on the page. â†³ Trust cues are most persuasive when they sit next to the CTA and reduce perceived risk at the exact decision moment. [Gold]

Source

Trust And Credibility, Hero Section Psychology
Open primary source ↗
TC-02MEDIUMTrust & Credibility
Social proof in the featured carousel feels patchy

Element

product-rating

Observation

Review signals appear inconsistently in the mobile featured carousel, and some proof sits partially out of view when only one full card and part of the next are visible at a time.

Recommendation

Standardize review coverage on featured cards and keep the most persuasive proof inside the first fully visible card frame.

Why this matters

Mobile shoppers cannot compare trust signals efficiently when the evidence is inconsistent and partially hidden by the carousel presentation. â†³ Review proof lifts confidence fastest when it is visible on the first comparison surface instead of being intermittent or buried. [Gold]

Source

Trust And Credibility, Product Cards
Open primary source ↗
PR-01MEDIUMPrice & Offers
Mobile product cards show price without enough delivery context

Element

made-to-order-badge

Observation

The carousel cards expose price and `Made to Order` badges, but they do not explain shipping thresholds, wait-time implications, or returns context, so mobile shoppers see cost without enough decision support.

Recommendation

Add compact mobile card copy for shipping threshold, expected build lead time on made-to-order items, and a short returns cue.

Why this matters

On mobile there is less room to infer tradeoffs, so missing value context makes prices feel riskier than they need to. â†³ Shipping and return framing often decide whether a displayed price feels fair or incomplete. [Gold]

Source

Pricing Psychology, Trust And Credibility
Open primary source ↗
CF-01MEDIUMCheckout Experience
Express-checkout reassurance is hidden until the footer

Element

payment-icons

Observation

Apple Pay, Shop Pay, and other payment methods are present in the footer area, but mobile shoppers do not encounter equivalent convenience messaging near the hero or first merch module.

Recommendation

Surface express-checkout and payment-trust messaging above the fold or directly above the first product carousel.

Why this matters

Early checkout reassurance reduces uncertainty on mobile, where friction expectations are already high and patience is low. â†³ Convenience cues perform best when shoppers see them before they commit to a deeper funnel path. [Silver]

Source

Trust And Credibility
Open primary source ↗
PU-01HIGHPerformance & UX
Mobile comparison starts too late and scans too slowly

Element

featured-grid

Observation

The first real product-comparison surface appears only after a tall hero stack, and then the featured module presents one full card plus a partial next card, which slows scan speed and comparison confidence.

Recommendation

Reduce the hero stack height and redesign the first merch module so multiple cues or stronger next-card visibility appear earlier in the mobile journey.

Why this matters

Mobile shoppers need to grasp choice and momentum quickly; long delays and narrow comparison windows make browsing feel heavier than it should. â†³ Faster comparison flows win on mobile because shoppers are far less willing to work through slow discovery patterns. [Gold]

Source

Hero Section Psychology, Product Cards
Open primary source ↗
PM-01MEDIUMProduct Photos & Video
Quick view and a single image do too much of the card's selling work

Element

quick-view-button

Observation

The mobile card presentation leans on a quick-view control and a single image rather than exposing stronger fitment confidence or richer context directly inside the product card.

Recommendation

Strengthen the first card view with better evidence on-card, such as clearer fitment relevance, stronger secondary image context, or a more informative product summary.

Why this matters

Mobile cards need to carry more explanatory load because every extra tap or modal interrupts the shopper's momentum. â†³ Better hierarchy and richer product-list context help users evaluate items without detouring into extra interactions. [Silver]

Source

Image Quantity Types, Product Cards
Open primary source ↗
CN-01MEDIUMBrowse & Search
Category routes appear after the fitment stack instead of accelerating it

Element

category-card

Observation

The mobile category cards do not appear until after the tall fitment flow, so they act as a second discovery layer instead of an early shortcut into relevant merch lanes.

Recommendation

Bring category shortcuts up sooner or let the fitment flow hand off directly into a `Shop your vehicle by category` experience.

Why this matters

Mobile shoppers benefit when routing choices accelerate discovery instead of waiting behind the page's heaviest interaction block. â†³ Filters and navigation perform better when they are promoted at the moment users are deciding how to browse, not after they have already worked through friction. [Silver]

Source

Collection Page Architecture, Search And Filter UX
Open primary source ↗
CS-01HIGHSearch Findability
Mobile still inherits weak homepage metadata and structure

Element

meta name="description"

Observation

The raw homepage HTML still carries the generic title `AWDMods - AWDMods`, no meta description, and no H1, which means the mobile entry point provides weak relevance signals to search and AI discovery systems.

Recommendation

Rewrite the homepage title and meta description around AWD performance parts and install a visible H1 aligned to the mobile hero promise.

Why this matters

Better metadata improves the quality of the click before mobile visitors ever land, and it keeps the homepage message consistent across acquisition channels. â†³ Generic titles are frequently rewritten, so a clearer structured promise is critical for discoverability. [Gold]

Source

Title Formulas SERP Psychology
Open primary source ↗
PP-01MEDIUMAfter Purchase
Lower-page retention still centers email capture over ownership confidence

Element

newsletter-input

Observation

The lower mobile experience emphasizes newsletter signup, but it does not give equal visibility to delivery confidence, support promises, referral momentum, or post-purchase help; `Track My Order` remains buried in footer navigation.

Recommendation

Recast the lower-page module around post-purchase confidence and ownership lifecycle value, then position email signup as a secondary follow-up action.

Why this matters

Buyers feel better about purchasing when they can picture the support and follow-up they will receive after conversion, not just the email list they will join. â†³ Post-purchase assurance lowers anxiety and strengthens repeat intent, making it more persuasive than capture-first messaging alone. [Silver]

Source

Post Purchase Psychology, Buyers Remorse
Open primary source ↗
AU-01HIGHAudience Fit
Mobile visitors are not told who the store is built for

Element

hero-fitment

Observation

The mobile hero collects detailed vehicle data, but it never proactively names the core AWD platforms, ownership goals, or buyer identities the store serves best before those fields appear.

Recommendation

Add a short audience-recognition line naming the key vehicle communities and the kind of owner AWDMods is built to help.

Why this matters

When the store recognizes the shopper's context first, the fitment flow feels like guidance instead of like unpaid classification work. â†³ Experience quality rises when visitors feel the site understands them before asking them to supply more data. [Silver]

Source

Hero Section Psychology, Personalization Psychology
Open primary source ↗
